fullTitle: MITRE ATT&CK Cloud Matrix (Enterprise ATT&CK — Cloud Platform)
description: The MITRE ATT&CK Cloud Matrix is a subset of the Enterprise ATT&CK framework focused on adversary tactics and techniques targeting cloud environments. Unlike the full Enterprise matrix, the Cloud matrix specifically covers techniques applicable to IaaS, SaaS, and Identity Provider platforms including AWS, Azure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP), Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and cloud identity services. It documents real-world adversary behaviour observed in cloud intrusions and provides a common language for threat modelling, detection engineering, and security assessments in cloud environments.
useInThisRepo: Each ATT&CK Cloud technique is mapped to CSA CCM v4 control domains that mitigate it, enabling defenders to navigate from known attack techniques to the controls that defend against them. This provides the adversary perspective for validating CCM control coverage and identifying gaps.

cloudPlatforms.name: Amazon Web Services (AWS)
cloudPlatforms.description: IaaS/PaaS platform — EC2, S3, IAM, Lambda, RDS, CloudTrail, GuardDuty

cloudPlatforms.name: Microsoft Azure
cloudPlatforms.description: IaaS/PaaS platform — VMs, Blob Storage, Azure AD/Entra ID, Functions, SQL Database, Azure Monitor

cloudPlatforms.name: Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
cloudPlatforms.description: IaaS/PaaS platform — Compute Engine, Cloud Storage, Cloud IAM, Cloud Functions, BigQuery, Cloud Audit Logs

cloudPlatforms.name: Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD)
cloudPlatforms.description: Identity Provider — SSO, MFA, Conditional Access, application registrations, service principals

cloudPlatforms.name: Microsoft 365
cloudPlatforms.description: SaaS platform — Exchange Online, SharePoint Online, OneDrive, Teams, Microsoft Purview

cloudPlatforms.name: Google Workspace
cloudPlatforms.description: SaaS platform — Gmail, Google Drive, Google Admin, Google Vault

cloudPlatforms.name: SaaS (Generic)
cloudPlatforms.description: General SaaS platform techniques applicable across multiple SaaS services

tactics.name: Initial Access
tactics.description: Techniques adversaries use to gain an initial foothold in a cloud environment, including compromised credentials, exploiting public-facing applications, and abusing trusted relationships

tactics.name: Execution
tactics.description: Techniques for running adversary-controlled code in cloud environments, including serverless function execution, cloud API abuse, and deployment of malicious workloads

tactics.name: Persistence
tactics.description: Techniques to maintain access to cloud environments across credential rotations and session expirations, including account creation, OAuth app registration, and role manipulation

tactics.name: Privilege Escalation
tactics.description: Techniques to gain higher-level permissions within cloud environments, including IAM policy manipulation, role assumption, and exploitation of overly permissive policies

tactics.name: Defense Evasion
tactics.description: Techniques to avoid detection in cloud environments, including disabling logging, modifying security configurations, and using legitimate cloud services for malicious purposes

tactics.name: Credential Access
tactics.description: Techniques for stealing cloud credentials including API keys, access tokens, service account keys, and secrets from metadata services or configuration stores

tactics.name: Discovery
tactics.description: Techniques for enumerating cloud infrastructure, permissions, storage, and services to understand the environment and identify targets

tactics.name: Lateral Movement
tactics.description: Techniques for moving between cloud services, accounts, subscriptions, and from cloud to on-premises environments

tactics.name: Collection
tactics.description: Techniques for gathering data from cloud storage, email services, databases, and other cloud-hosted repositories

tactics.name: Exfiltration
tactics.description: Techniques for extracting data from cloud environments, including transfer to attacker-controlled cloud accounts and abuse of cloud storage sharing features

tactics.name: Impact
tactics.description: Techniques for disrupting cloud service availability, destroying data, or hijacking cloud resources for adversary purposes such as cryptomining

keyIncidentMappings.description: SSRF vulnerability in WAF exploited to access EC2 instance metadata, assume IAM role, and exfiltrate 100M+ customer records from S3.
After on-premises compromise, adversary forged SAML tokens to access Microsoft 365 and Azure AD, created backdoor service principals, and exfiltrated emails.
Compromised CI/CD tool exfiltrated environment variables containing cloud credentials, API keys, and tokens from thousands of customer build environments.
Adversary obtained Microsoft signing key, forged Azure AD tokens to access Exchange Online mailboxes of government agencies.
Social engineering of help desks for MFA resets, followed by Azure AD persistence, privilege escalation, and ransomware deployment in cloud environments.

relationToEnterprise: The Cloud matrix is not a separate ATT&CK matrix — it is a filtered view of Enterprise ATT&CK showing only techniques with cloud platform applicability. Technique IDs are shared with the Enterprise matrix (e.g., T1078 Valid Accounts appears in both). Some techniques are cloud-exclusive (e.g., T1535 Unused/Unsupported Cloud Regions), while others are shared with on-premises contexts.
